summary: This is a brief link roundup post where Joel curates reactions and related commentary from other bloggers on topics related to startups, venture capital, and business agreements. He highlights Joi Ito on no-shop agreements, John Ludwig on VC spending advice, Zimran Ahmed on entrepreneurial overconfidence, and Eric Sink on the limitations of VC firms for building lasting companies.
key_points: No-shop agreements can help both parties focus on building a business when a deal requires significant work before closing
VCs don't typically encourage startups to spend more and faster, contrary to popular belief
Entrepreneurs tend to overestimate the certainty of their 'sure thing' opportunities
Most VC firms aren't well-suited for founders who want to build solid, long-lived companies
money_quotes: I don't think I've ever heard anyone at our firm telling a startup to spend more, faster.
…those 'sure things' that Joel mentions exist more often in the mind of the entrepreneur than in reality.
… most VC firms don't have much to offer people who want to build solid, long-lived companies.
key_insight: A collection of outside perspectives challenging common assumptions about venture capital, startup spending, and entrepreneurial confidence.
